text: The validity of the continuum assumption may be verified by a theoretical analysis, in which either some clear periodicity is identified or statistical homogeneity and ergodicity of the microstructure exist. More specifically, the continuum hypothesis hinges on the concepts of a representative elementary volume and separation of scales based on the Hill–Mandel condition. This condition provides a link between an experimentalist's and a theoretician's viewpoint on constitutive equations (linear and nonlinear elastic/inelastic or coupled fields) as well as a way of spatial and statistical averaging of the microstructure. When the separation of scales does not hold, or when one wants to establish a continuum of a finer resolution than the size of the representative volume element (RVE), a statistical volume element (SVE) is employed, which results in random continuum fields. The latter then provide a micromechanics basis for stochastic finite elements (SFE). The levels of SVE and RVE link continuum mechanics to statistical mechanics. Experimentally, the RVE can only be evaluated when the constitutive response is spatially homogenous.

text: Apollo 8 was a NASA spaceflight mission that launched on December 21, 1968, and successfully completed the first manned lunar orbit. After launching from Kennedy Space Center's Launch Complex 39A, the spacecraft entered into Earth orbit before trans-lunar injection, which placed it on course for the Moon. The crew entered into lunar orbit on Christmas Eve and spent approximately 21 hours in orbit around the Moon, conducting scientific experiments and taking photographs of the lunar surface. During their time in orbit, the astronauts also performed a series of maneuvers to test the spacecraft's systems and prepare for future manned missions to the Moon. text: Clipperton Island is a small uninhabited coral atoll located in the eastern Pacific Ocean. It is situated approximately 43 kilometers southwest of the Revillagigedo Islands and about 1,100 kilometers southwest of Mexico's Baja California Peninsula. The island has a total land area of approximately 8 square kilometers and is surrounded by a coral reef that provides habitat for a variety of marine life, including sea turtles, sharks, and tropical fish. Clipperton Island was discovered in 1535 by the Spanish conquistador Thomas de la Vega, who named it after John Clipperton, an English pirate who had previously visited the island. The atoll has been uninhabited since the early 20th century due to its remote location and lack of fresh water resources. text: Alboin was probably born in the 530s in Pannonia, the son of Audoin and his wife, Rodelinda. Like his father, Alboin was raised a pagan, although Audoin had at one point attempted to gain Byzantine support against his neighbours by professing himself a Christian. Alboin took as his first wife the Christian Chlothsind, daughter of the Frankish King Chlothar. This marriage, which took place soon after the death of the Frankish ruler Theudebald in 555, is thought to reflect Audoin's decision to distance himself from the Byzantines, traditional allies of the Lombards, who had been lukewarm when it came to supporting Audoin against the Gepids. The new Frankish alliance was important because of the Franks' known hostility to the Byzantine empire, providing the Lombards with more than one option. However, the Prosopography of the Later Roman Empire interprets events and sources differently, believing that Alboin married Chlothsind when already a king in or shortly before 561, the year of Chlothar's death.

text: The alkali metals are a group of elements in the periodic table that belong to Group 1. They are characterized by their single valence electron, which is easily lost to form a positive ion with a +1 charge. The six elements that comprise this group are lithium (Li), sodium (Na), potassium (K), rubidium (Rb), caesium (Cs), and francium (Fr). These metals are highly reactive due to their low ionization energy, which makes them readily lose electrons to form compounds with other elements. They are also highly electropositive, meaning they tend to donate electrons rather than gain them. The alkali metals have a range of physical properties, including varying levels of reactivity and melting points. Their chemical properties are similar, however, as they all exhibit the same +1 oxidation state in their compounds. Overall, the alkali metals play an important role in various industrial applications, such as the production of batteries and other chemicals.

text: Transition to iron Though bronze is generally harder than wrought iron, with Vickers hardness of 60–258 vs. 30–80, the Bronze Age gave way to the Iron Age after a serious disruption of the tin trade: the population migrations of around 1200–1100 BCE reduced the shipping of tin around the Mediterranean and from Britain, limiting supplies and raising prices. As the art of working in iron improved, iron became cheaper and improved in quality. As cultures advanced from hand-wrought iron to machine-forged iron (typically made with trip hammers powered by water), blacksmiths learned how to make steel. Steel is stronger and harder than bronze and holds a sharper edge longer.

text: History Batters have not been charged with a time at-bat for a sacrifice hit since 1893, but baseball has changed the sacrifice fly rule multiple times. The sacrifice fly as a statistical category was instituted in 1908, only to be discontinued in 1931. The rule was again adopted in 1939, only to be eliminated again in 1940, before being adopted for the last time in 1954. For some baseball fans, it is significant that the sacrifice-fly rule was eliminated in 1940 because, in 1941, Ted Williams was hitting .39955 on the last day of the season and needed one hit in a doubleheader against the Philadelphia A's to become the first hitter since Bill Terry in 1930 to hit .400. He got six hits, finishing with an official .406 average, the last player in over 80 years to bat .400 or more in the American or National League. In his book Baseball and Other Matters in 1941 author Robert Creamer, citing estimates, points out that if Williams' 14 at-bats on sacrifice flies that year were deducted from the 456 official at-bats he was charged with, his final average in 1941 would have been .419.

The series heated up recently when former Jets head coach Rex Ryan became the Bills' head coach for two seasons, and had become notable again as Bills quarterback Josh Allen and former Jets quarterback Sam Darnold, both drafted in the same year, maintained a friendly rivalry with one another. text: Brussels has had a distinguished artist scene for many years. The famous Belgian surrealists René Magritte and Paul Delvaux, for instance, studied and lived there, as did the avant-garde dramatist Michel de Ghelderode. The city was also home of the impressionist painter Anna Boch from the artists' group Les XX, and includes other famous Belgian painters such as Léon Spilliaert. Brussels is also a capital of the comic strip; some treasured Belgian characters are Tintin, Lucky Luke, The Smurfs, Spirou, Gaston, Marsupilami, Blake and Mortimer, Boule et Bill and Cubitus (see Belgian comics). Throughout the city, walls are painted with large motifs of comic book characters; these murals taken together are known as Brussels' Comic Book Route. Also, the interiors of some Metro stations are designed by artists. The Belgian Comic Strip Center combines two artistic leitmotifs of Brussels, being a museum devoted to Belgian comic strips, housed in the former Magasins Waucquez textile department store, designed by Victor Horta in the Art Nouveau style. In addition, street art is changing the landscape of this multicultural city.

text: Limited animation involves the use of less detailed or more stylized drawings and methods of movement usually a choppy or "skippy" movement animation. Limited animation uses fewer drawings per second, thereby limiting the fluidity of the animation. This is a more economic technique. Pioneered by the artists at the American studio United Productions of America, limited animation can be used as a method of stylized artistic expression, as in Gerald McBoing-Boing (US, 1951), Yellow Submarine (UK, 1968), and certain anime produced in Japan. Its primary use, however, has been in producing cost-effective animated content for media for television (the work of Hanna-Barbera, Filmation, and other TV animation studios) and later the Internet (web cartoons).

text: The idea of creating a chess-playing machine dates to the 18th century; around 1769, the chess-playing automaton called The Turk became famous before being exposed as a hoax. Serious trials based on automata, such as El Ajedrecista, were too complex and limited to be useful. Since the advent of the digital computer in the 1950s, chess enthusiasts, computer engineers, and computer scientists have built, with increasing degrees of seriousness and success, chess-playing machines and computer programs. The groundbreaking paper on computer chess, "Programming a Computer for Playing Chess", was published in 1950 by Claude Shannon. He wrote: The chess machine is an ideal one to start with, since: (1) the problem is sharply defined both in allowed operations (the moves) and in the ultimate goal (checkmate); (2) it is neither so simple as to be trivial nor too difficult for satisfactory solution; (3) chess is generally considered to require "thinking" for skillful play; a solution of this problem will force us either to admit the possibility of a mechanized thinking or to further restrict our concept of "thinking"; (4) the discrete structure of chess fits well into the digital nature of modern computers.

text: A ballad is a form of narrative poetry or song that typically tells a story in verse. It often has a folkloric or legendary theme and may be based on historical events or myths. The narrative is often presented in a straightforward manner, with minimal embellishment or ornamentation. Ballads may be sung or recited, and their melodies are frequently simple and memorable. They have been an important part of oral traditions for centuries, serving as a means of passing down stories and cultural heritage from one generation to the next. text: This translation of the Kitáb-i-ʻAhd is based on a solecism, however, as the terms Akbar and Aʻzam do not mean, respectively, 'Greater' and 'Most Great'. Not only do the two words derive from entirely separate triconsonantal roots (Akbar from k-b-r and Aʻzam from ʻ-z-m), but the Arabic language possesses the elative, a stage of gradation, with no clear distinction between the comparative and superlative. In the Will and Testament ʻAbdu'l-Bahá's half-brother, Muhammad ʻAlí, was mentioned by name as being subordinate to ʻAbdu'l-Bahá. Muhammad ʻAlí became jealous of his half-brother and set out to establish authority for himself as an alternative leader with the support of his brothers Badiʻu'llah and Ḍíyáʼu'llah. He began correspondence with Baháʼís in Iran, initially in secret, casting doubts in others' minds about ʻAbdu'l-Bahá. text: Bronze is a hard, silvery-brown alloy of copper and tin. It has been used by humans for various purposes since ancient times, including the production of tools, weapons, and decorative items. The earliest known bronze artifacts date back to around 3000 BCE in Mesopotamia, where it was likely discovered as a natural occurrence. Bronze's unique properties make it an ideal material for crafting durable objects: it is stronger than copper but more malleable than iron, allowing for intricate designs and shapes. The addition of tin to copper creates a stable compound that resists corrosion and maintains its shape over time. As civilizations developed, bronze became a widely used material in various cultures, including ancient Egypt, Greece, and Rome. Its widespread adoption was likely due to the ease with which it could be cast into desired forms using simple techniques. The use of bronze continued well into the Middle Ages before being largely replaced by iron and steel.

text: Conscription is controversial for a range of reasons, including conscientious objection to military engagements on religious or philosophical grounds; political objection, for example to service for a disliked government or unpopular war; sexism, in that historically men have been subject to the draft in the most cases; and ideological objection, for example, to a perceived violation of individual rights. Those conscripted may evade service, sometimes by leaving the country, and seeking asylum in another country. Some selection systems accommodate these attitudes by providing alternative service outside combat-operations roles or even outside the military, such as (alternative civil service) in Finland, (compulsory community service) in Austria, Germany and Switzerland. Several countries conscript male soldiers not only for armed forces, but also for paramilitary agencies, which are dedicated to police-like domestic only service like internal troops, border guards or non-combat rescue duties like civil defence.

The French effort required five TBMs: two main marine machines, one mainland machine (the short land drives of allowed one TBM to complete the first drive then reverse direction and complete the other), and two service tunnel machines. On the English side, the simpler geology allowed faster open-faced TBMs. Six machines were used; all commenced digging from Shakespeare Cliff, three marine-bound and three for the land tunnels. Towards the completion of the undersea drives, the UK TBMs were driven steeply downwards and buried clear of the tunnel. These buried TBMs were then used to provide an electrical earth. The French TBMs then completed the tunnel and were dismantled. A gauge railway was used on the English side during construction.

text: Character encoding is a system used to represent characters from various languages using binary code. It assigns a unique numerical value to each character, allowing computers and other devices to process and display text accurately. The most common character encodings are based on the ASCII (American Standard Code for Information Interchange) standard, which defines 128 unique values for characters in the English alphabet. However, as languages with non-English scripts became more prevalent, new encoding schemes were developed to accommodate these characters. Unicode is a widely used character encoding that supports over 100,000 characters from various languages and scripts, including Chinese, Japanese, Korean, Arabic, and many others. Unicode uses a combination of code points and code units to represent each character, allowing for greater flexibility and compatibility across different devices and platforms. As a result, character encoding has become an essential aspect of modern computing, enabling the efficient representation and display of text in diverse languages and scripts.
label: llm

text: The history of baseball in the United States dates back to the mid-19th century when it evolved from various bat-and-ball games played by English and Irish immigrants. The modern game began to take shape in 1845 with the establishment of the New York Knickerbockers, one of the first organized baseball teams. In 1857, the first official rules were published, and the game gained popularity throughout the country. The Civil War era saw a surge in interest, with the formation of professional teams and leagues. The National League was founded in 1876, followed by the American League in 1901, which led to the creation of the modern Major League Baseball (MLB) structure. The early 20th century saw the rise of legendary players like Babe Ruth and Lou Gehrig, while the mid-century era introduced Jackie Robinson as a trailblazer for racial integration. Since then, baseball has continued to evolve with advancements in technology, changes in player demographics, and shifts in fan culture.

text: The Council of Europe is a pan-European organization that aims to promote democracy, human rights, and the rule of law among its member states. Established in 1949, it has 47 member countries, including all European countries except Belarus and Vatican City. The council's main activities include promoting cultural exchange, protecting human rights, and combating crime through international cooperation. It also provides a platform for member states to discuss and address common challenges such as migration, terrorism, and climate change. The Council of Europe is headquartered in Strasbourg, France, where its parliamentary assembly meets regularly to debate and adopt resolutions on various issues. The council's institutions include the European Court of Human Rights, which interprets the European Convention on Human Rights, and the European Commission against Racism and Intolerance, which monitors and combats racism and xenophobia. Overall, the Council of Europe plays a significant role in promoting cooperation and understanding among its member states.

text: Ukridge's Accident Syndicate is a fictional organization created by P.G. Wodehouse in his series of novels and short stories featuring the character Reggie Pepper. The syndicate is a group of individuals who pool their resources to provide financial assistance to each other in times of need, particularly when accidents or mishaps occur. Members of the syndicate are expected to contribute a set amount of money on a regular basis, which is then used to compensate those who have suffered losses due to unforeseen events. The syndicate's rules and regulations are often humorous and satirical, reflecting Wodehouse's comedic style. Ukridge, one of the main characters associated with the syndicate, is known for his eccentric behavior and tendency to get into trouble, which frequently results in him being on the receiving end of financial assistance from the syndicate. Through the concept of the Accident Syndicate, Wodehouse pokes fun at the idea of mutual aid societies and the human tendency to rely on others in times of need.

text: Ada Lovelace was a British mathematician and writer who is often considered the world's first computer programmer. Born Augusta Ada Byron in 1815, she was the daughter of the poet Lord George Gordon Byron and his wife Anne Isabella Milbanke. Despite her family's aristocratic background, Lovelace's education focused on mathematics and science, which was unusual for a woman at that time. She developed a close relationship with mathematician Charles Babbage, who was working on the Analytical Engine, an early mechanical computer. Lovelace translated an article on the engine by Italian mathematician Luigi Menabrea, adding her own notes and comments to the translation. Her notes, particularly those on the potential of the machine to go beyond mere calculation and perform any task that could be expressed in a series of steps, are now recognized as a description of a computer program. Lovelace's work on the Analytical Engine was published in 1843, making her the first person to recognize the potential of computers to go beyond mere calculation.

text: China is a country located in East Asia, bordered by the Pacific Ocean to the east, Mongolia and Kazakhstan to the north, Nepal, Bhutan, India, Myanmar, Laos, and Vietnam to the south, and Russia to the northeast. With a total area of approximately 9.6 million square kilometers, it is the third-largest country in the world by land area. China has a diverse geography, featuring mountains, deserts, and plateaus, with the Himalayas to the southwest and the Tian Shan mountain range to the northwest. The climate varies greatly across the country, ranging from tropical in the south to temperate in the north. With a population of over 1.4 billion people, China is the most populous country in the world. Its capital city is Beijing, while Shanghai is its largest city and major financial hub. China has a long history dating back thousands of years, with various dynasties rising and falling throughout its history, including the Qin, Han, Tang, Song, Ming, and Qing dynasties.

text: Charles Babbage was an English mathematician and inventor who is widely regarded as the father of computer science. Born in 1791, he studied mathematics at Trinity College, Cambridge, where he developed a passion for mathematical analysis and machine design. In the early 19th century, Babbage designed two mechanical general-purpose computers: the Difference Engine, intended to automate mathematical calculations, and the Analytical Engine, a more advanced machine capable of performing any calculation using punched cards and a central processing unit. Although neither machine was completed during his lifetime due to funding issues, Babbage's ideas laid the foundation for modern computer design. His work on the Analytical Engine also introduced concepts such as programming languages, input/output devices, and memory storage, which are still fundamental to computing today. Despite his contributions to the development of computer science, Babbage's legacy was largely forgotten until the 20th century, when his ideas were rediscovered and recognized for their significance in shaping the modern computer industry.

text: Artillery refers to a type of military weapon designed to launch projectiles over long distances. It typically consists of a gun or howitzer mounted on wheels or a tracked vehicle, and is used by armies to attack enemy positions from a distance. Artillery can be categorized into several types, including field artillery, which is mobile and often deployed in the field; siege artillery, which is heavier and designed for breaching fortifications; and anti-aircraft artillery, which is used to defend against aerial attacks. The projectiles fired by artillery can vary greatly in size, shape, and type, ranging from small shells to large howitzer rounds. Modern artillery also includes guided munitions, such as precision-guided bombs and rockets, which use advanced technology to increase their accuracy and effectiveness. Artillery has been a crucial component of military forces throughout history, with its development influencing the course of many conflicts and wars.

text: The caspase proteins are integral parts of the apoptosis pathway, so it follows that knock-outs made have varying damaging results. A caspase 9 knock-out leads to a severe brain malformation . A caspase 8 knock-out leads to cardiac failure and thus embryonic lethality . However, with the use of cre-lox technology, a caspase 8 knock-out has been created that exhibits an increase in peripheral T cells, an impaired T cell response, and a defect in neural tube closure . These mice were found to be resistant to apoptosis mediated by CD95, TNFR, etc. but not resistant to apoptosis caused by UV irradiation, chemotherapeutic drugs, and other stimuli. Finally, a caspase 3 knock-out was characterized by ectopic cell masses in the brain and abnormal apoptotic features such as membrane blebbing or nuclear fragmentation . A remarkable feature of these KO mice is that they have a very restricted phenotype: Casp3, 9, APAF-1 KO mice have deformations of neural tissue and FADD and Casp 8 KO showed defective heart development, however, in both types of KO other organs developed normally and some cell types were still sensitive to apoptotic stimuli suggesting that unknown proapoptotic pathways exist.

text: Axiology is the branch of philosophy that deals with the study and evaluation of values. It encompasses various aspects of value theory, including the nature, origin, and significance of values in human life. Axiologists examine the concepts of good, bad, right, wrong, beautiful, and ugly to understand how they are perceived and applied across different cultures and societies. The field also explores the relationship between values and other philosophical disciplines such as ethics, aesthetics, and epistemology. Axiological inquiry can be divided into two main areas: descriptive axiology, which seeks to describe and classify existing values, and normative axiology, which aims to establish standards for evaluating and justifying values. By analyzing and comparing different value systems, axiologists aim to provide a deeper understanding of the complex and multifaceted nature of human values, their role in shaping individual and collective behavior, and their impact on society as a whole.

text: The Apatosaurus is a genus of sauropod dinosaurs that lived during the Late Jurassic period, around 155 to 145 million years ago. It was a large herbivorous animal, characterized by its long neck and tail, and four columnar legs. The name "Apatosaurus" means "deceptive lizard," which refers to the fact that it was initially thought to be a type of lizard due to its scaly skin impressions found in fossilized tracks. However, further study revealed that these impressions were likely from other animals or plant material. Apatosaurs had a distinctive set of vertebrae in their neck and tail, with specialized ribs that allowed for greater flexibility and movement. They are known from several well-preserved fossils found in North America, including the famous "Elvis" fossil discovered in Colorado. The discovery of these fossils has provided valuable insights into the biology and behavior of Apatosaurs, which likely lived in herds and used their long necks to feed on high-growing vegetation.

The channel provides continuous coverage of domestic and international news, with a focus on in-depth analysis and live reporting from around the world. Programming includes news bulletins, current affairs shows, and documentaries, as well as coverage of major events such as elections, natural disasters, and conflicts. BBC News is available to viewers in the United Kingdom and internationally through various platforms, including satellite television, cable, and online streaming services. The channel's content is also available on demand through the BBC iPlayer service, allowing users to access news programs from previous days or weeks. He released his debut single, "Bo Diddley," in 1955, which reached the top of the Billboard charts and established him as a major figure in early rock and roll. Throughout his career, Bo Diddley released numerous albums and singles, collaborating with other notable musicians such as Chuck Berry and Little Richard. text: Human cannibalism is a practice in which humans consume the flesh of other humans. It has been documented throughout history and across various cultures, often in times of extreme scarcity or during periods of social upheaval. In some societies, cannibalism was considered a ritualistic act, associated with spiritual or ceremonial practices. For example, certain tribes in Papua New Guinea believed that consuming the flesh of their enemies would transfer their strength and power. In other cases, cannibalism was practiced as a means of survival, particularly during times of famine or war when traditional food sources were scarce. Historical accounts also suggest that cannibalism occurred among some European explorers and colonizers in the Americas, who resorted to consuming human flesh due to extreme hunger and desperation. The practice of human cannibalism has been largely stigmatized and is now widely considered taboo in most cultures around the world.
label: llm

text: Assembly language is a low-level programming language that uses symbolic representations of machine code instructions to communicate with the computer's processor. It is designed to be translated directly into machine code by an assembler, which converts the assembly code into binary code that the processor can execute. Assembly languages are specific to a particular computer architecture and operating system, meaning that assembly code written for one platform will not run on another without modification. Despite its low-level nature, assembly language provides direct access to hardware resources such as memory addresses, registers, and input/output devices, making it a popular choice for systems programming, embedded systems development, and applications requiring high performance or low-level control. The use of symbolic representations in assembly language makes it easier to write and maintain code compared to machine code, but it also requires the programmer to have a deep understanding of the computer's architecture and instruction set.
label: llm

text: An anemometer is a device used to measure wind speed and direction. It typically consists of a series of cups or vanes that rotate in response to the movement of air. The rotation is then converted into a digital reading using sensors and electronics, providing accurate measurements of wind velocity and direction. Anemometers are commonly used in meteorology for weather forecasting and research purposes, as well as in various industrial applications such as wind energy generation and aerodynamics testing. They can be installed at ground level or elevated on towers to measure wind patterns over different terrain types and altitudes. The data collected by an anemometer is essential for understanding local climate conditions, predicting storms and weather events, and optimizing the performance of wind turbines and other wind-powered systems. Anemometers are available in various designs and sizes, ranging from small handheld models to large, complex installations used in research stations and meteorological observatories.

text: Ansible is an open-source software tool used for configuration management and deployment of applications across a network. Developed by Red Hat, it allows users to define the desired state of their infrastructure and automatically enforce that state on remote servers. Ansible achieves this through its use of playbooks, which are written in YAML or Ansible's own syntax, and contain instructions for configuring and deploying software. These playbooks can be executed remotely using a connection protocol such as SSH, allowing users to manage multiple machines from a single location. Ansible also supports inventory management, enabling users to track the status and configuration of their infrastructure. The tool is widely used in DevOps environments due to its ease of use and flexibility, making it a popular choice for automating tasks such as deployment, scaling, and maintenance of applications across various platforms. Its capabilities have led to adoption by numerous organizations, including those in the fields of finance, healthcare, and technology.

text: The successful metal pressure tube anemometer of William Henry Dines in 1892 utilized the same pressure difference between the open mouth of a straight tube facing the wind and a ring of small holes in a vertical tube which is closed at the upper end. Both are mounted at the same height. The pressure differences on which the action depends are very small, and special means are required to register them. The recorder consists of a float in a sealed chamber partially filled with water. The pipe from the straight tube is connected to the top of the sealed chamber and the pipe from the small tubes is directed into the bottom inside the float. Since the pressure difference determines the vertical position of the float this is a measure of the wind speed.

text: Inflation is a hypothetical period of extremely rapid expansion in the early universe, proposed to explain various features and phenomena observed in the cosmic microwave background radiation. According to this theory, the universe underwent an exponential growth spurt in the first fraction of a second after the Big Bang, smoothing out any irregularities and explaining why the universe appears so homogeneous on large scales. This inflationary period would have occurred when the universe was still incredibly hot and dense, with energies far beyond those that can be replicated in laboratory experiments. The inflationary model predicts that the universe is much larger than what can be observed directly, and that the cosmic microwave background radiation is a remnant of this early expansion. While the concept of inflation remains highly speculative, it has been widely adopted as a framework for understanding the evolution of the universe on very large scales, and has been supported by various observations and simulations.

text: Aldous Huxley was a British novelist, philosopher, and critic. Born on July 26, 1894, in Godalming, England, he came from a prominent family of intellectuals. His father, Leonard Huxley, was a biologist and editor, while his brother, Julian Huxley, was also a biologist. Aldous studied English literature at Balliol College, Oxford, where he developed an interest in philosophy and psychology. He began writing novels in the 1920s, with his first published work being "Crome Yellow" (1921). His most famous novel, "Brave New World," was published in 1932 and explores themes of totalitarianism, consumerism, and the effects of advanced technology on society. Huxley's other notable works include "Eyeless in Gaza" (1936) and "The Doors of Perception" (1954), a non-fiction book that describes his experiences with mescaline. text: Chromium(VI) toxicity The acute oral toxicity for chromium(VI) ranges between 1.5 and 3.3 mg/kg. In the body, chromium(VI) is reduced by several mechanisms to chromium(III) already in the blood before it enters the cells. The chromium(III) is excreted from the body, whereas the chromate ion is transferred into the cell by a transport mechanism, by which also sulfate and phosphate ions enter the cell. The acute toxicity of chromium(VI) is due to its strong oxidant properties. After it reaches the blood stream, it damages the kidneys, the liver and blood cells through oxidation reactions. Hemolysis, renal, and liver failure result. Aggressive dialysis can be therapeutic.

text: The Battle of Ramillies was a significant engagement fought on May 23, 1706, during the War of the Spanish Succession. The battle took place near the town of Ramillies in what is now the Walloon region of Belgium. A combined force of British and Dutch troops under the command of the Duke of Marlborough clashed with a French army led by the Duke of Villeroi. The French forces were attempting to relieve the siege of the fortress city of Namur, which was being besieged by Allied forces. However, Marlborough's forces successfully outmaneuvered the French and launched a surprise attack on their right flank, causing significant casualties and forcing Villeroi to retreat. The battle resulted in a decisive victory for the Allies, with estimates suggesting that they suffered around 5,000 casualties compared to the French losses of approximately 13,000 men. text: Substantive theories of value focus on the features in virtue of which something has intrinsic value. Popular candidates for these features include pleasure, virtue and knowledge. The main approaches to this question can be divided into the Kantian tradition, which considers concrete things like persons to be the bearers of value, and the Moorean tradition, which holds that only states of affairs bear value. This difference is important when determining whether a value is extrinsic or intrinsic to an entity. Some philosophers hold that objects like Napoleon's hat are valuable because of their relation to extraordinary persons. From a Kantian perspective, this value must be extrinsic since it is based on the extrinsic property of having been worn by an extraordinary person. But from a Moorean perspective, it can be intrinsic since it is born not by the hat but by a state of affairs involving both the hat and Napoleon.

To the southwest it is adjoined by the Black Forest Grinden and Enz Hills (, 151), along the upper reaches of the Enz and Murg, forming the heart of the Northern Black Forest. The west of the Northern Black Forest is formed by the Northern Black Forest Valleys (, 152) with the middle reaches of the Murg around Gernsbach, the middle course of the Oos to Baden-Baden, the middle reaches of the Bühlot above Bühls and the upper reaches of the Rench around Oppenau. text: Optimised bytecode Weak typing in APL means that a name may reference an array (of any datatype), a function or an operator. In general, the interpreter cannot know in advance which form it will be and must therefore perform analysis, syntax checking etc. at run-time. However, in certain circumstances, it is possible to deduce in advance what type a name is expected to reference and then generate bytecode which can be executed with reduced run-time overhead. This bytecode can also be optimised using compilation techniques such as constant folding or common subexpression elimination. The interpreter will execute the bytecode when present and when any assumptions which have been made are met. Dyalog APL includes support for optimised bytecode.

text: Absolute zero is the theoretical temperature at which all matter would have zero entropy, meaning that all molecular motion would cease. It is defined as 0 Kelvin (K), -273.15 degrees Celsius (°C), or -459.67 degrees Fahrenheit (°F). This temperature is considered a fundamental limit in physics and has been the subject of extensive research and experimentation. In practice, it is impossible to achieve absolute zero due to the third law of thermodynamics, which states that as the temperature of a system approaches absolute zero, the entropy of the system will also approach a minimum value, but never actually reach it. As a result, scientists have been able to cool materials to extremely low temperatures, such as near-absolute zero, using advanced techniques like laser cooling and magnetic levitation, but true absolute zero remains an unattainable goal. The concept of absolute zero has significant implications for fields such as cryogenics, superconductivity, and quantum mechanics.

text: The beaver is a large, semi-aquatic rodent native to North America and Europe. It belongs to the family Castoridae and is one of the largest rodents in the world. These structures can be up to 10 feet wide and have multiple entrances and exits. text: Athanasius of Alexandria was a Christian theologian and bishop who played a significant role in the development of early Christianity. Born around 296 AD in Alexandria, Egypt, he studied under the tutelage of Bishop Alexander, who would later become his predecessor as Patriarch of Alexandria. In 328 AD, Athanasius succeeded Alexander as Patriarch, a position he held until his death in 373 AD. He was a key figure in the Arian controversy, which centered on the nature of Christ's divinity. Athanasius advocated for the orthodox view that Jesus Christ is fully divine and co-eternal with God the Father, while Arius argued that Christ was a created being subordinate to God. The Council of Nicaea in 325 AD, convened by Emperor Constantine, officially condemned Arianism, but the controversy continued to simmer throughout Athanasius's lifetime. Despite facing exile and persecution, Athanasius remained committed to his theological convictions and wrote extensively on the nature of Christ, producing influential works such as "On the Incarnation" and "Against the Arians".

text: It is often impractical to store and use poisonous chlorine gas for water treatment, so alternative methods of adding chlorine are used. These include hypochlorite solutions, which gradually release chlorine into the water, and compounds like sodium dichloro-s-triazinetrione (dihydrate or anhydrous), sometimes referred to as "dichlor", and trichloro-s-triazinetrione, sometimes referred to as "trichlor". These compounds are stable while solid and may be used in powdered, granular, or tablet form. When added in small amounts to pool water or industrial water systems, the chlorine atoms hydrolyze from the rest of the molecule, forming hypochlorous acid (HOCl), which acts as a general biocide, killing germs, microorganisms, algae, and so on.

Abdera was also an important cultural hub, known for its rich literary and philosophical heritage. Notably, the city was the birthplace of the ancient Greek philosopher Protagoras, who is considered one of the founders of Western philosophy. The city's ruins have been extensively excavated, revealing a well-planned urban layout with impressive architectural features, including a theater and several temples dedicated to various deities. text: A computer fan is a device used to cool electronic components within a computer system. It operates by drawing in air from the surrounding environment and expelling it after passing over a heat sink or other cooling surface. This process helps to dissipate heat generated by the computer's internal components, such as the central processing unit (CPU) and graphics card. Computer fans are typically powered by electricity and can be controlled by the computer's motherboard or a separate fan controller. They come in various shapes and sizes, including axial fans that draw air in parallel with their axis of rotation and radial fans that draw air in from the sides. The speed at which a computer fan operates is often adjustable, allowing users to balance cooling performance against noise levels. In addition to passive heat sinks, computer fans are an essential component for maintaining optimal operating temperatures within a computer system.

text: Anarchism is a political philosophy that advocates for the abolition of all forms of government and authority. It emphasizes individual freedom and autonomy, rejecting the idea of a centralized power structure. Anarchists argue that institutions such as governments, laws, and social hierarchies are unnecessary and often oppressive, instead advocating for voluntary associations and direct action to achieve social change. The concept of anarchism has its roots in ancient Greece and Rome, but it gained significant attention during the 19th-century European revolutions. Key figures associated with anarchist thought include Pierre-Joseph Proudhon, Mikhail Bakunin, and Emma Goldman. Anarchist movements have been diverse, ranging from pacifist and utopian to revolutionary and militant. While some anarchists advocate for a gradual transition to a stateless society through education and social reform, others propose more radical approaches, such as violent revolution or direct action against existing power structures.

text: Blood alcohol content (BAC) refers to the concentration of alcohol in a person's blood. It is typically measured as a percentage by volume and is usually expressed in terms of grams per 100 milliliters of blood. The BAC can be influenced by various factors, including the amount and rate at which an individual consumes alcohol, their body weight, sex, age, and metabolism. In general, a person's BAC will rise as they consume more alcohol, but it will also decrease over time due to the body's natural processes for eliminating alcohol. The effects of alcohol on the body can vary depending on the individual's BAC level, with higher levels typically associated with increased impairment and risk of adverse consequences such as accidents, injuries, or health problems. In many jurisdictions, laws are in place that specify a maximum allowable BAC limit for operating vehicles or engaging in other activities that require coordination and attention.

text: A chemical element is a substance that consists of atoms with the same number of protons in the atomic nucleus. This number, known as the atomic number, determines the identity and properties of an element. Elements are the simplest substances in chemistry and cannot be broken down into simpler substances by chemical means. They can exist in various forms, such as solids, liquids, or gases, depending on their physical state. The elements are typically denoted by a one- or two-letter symbol, which is derived from their name or a related word. There are currently 118 known elements, ranging from hydrogen (atomic number 1) to oganesson (atomic number 118). Elements can be classified into metals, nonmetals, and metalloids based on their properties and electron configuration. The discovery of new elements has been an ongoing process throughout history, with the most recent additions being synthetic elements created in laboratories.

text: Copyright is a form of intellectual property law that grants exclusive rights to creators and owners of original works. It allows them to control how their work is used, distributed, and reproduced. Copyright protection typically applies to literary, dramatic, musical, and artistic works, as well as software and other digital content. The duration of copyright varies depending on the jurisdiction, but it generally lasts for a specified number of years after the creator's death or the publication date. In many countries, copyright is automatic, meaning that creators do not need to register their work to obtain protection. However, registration can provide additional benefits, such as proof of ownership and priority in case of disputes. Copyright laws also include provisions for fair use, which allows limited use of copyrighted material without permission from the owner, typically for purposes such as criticism, commentary, news reporting, teaching, scholarship, or research.

text: The Central African Republic has a history dating back to ancient times, with archaeological evidence suggesting human habitation as far back as 2,000 BCE. The region was later inhabited by various ethnic groups, including the Sara and the M'Baka, who developed complex societies and trading networks. In the late 19th century, European powers began to exert influence over the area, with France establishing a protectorate in 1894. The country gained independence from France on August 13, 1960, with David Dacko becoming its first president. However, his rule was short-lived, and he was overthrown by Jean-Bedel Bokassa in 1966. Bokassa declared himself emperor in 1976, but his regime was marked by authoritarianism and human rights abuses. He was eventually ousted in a French-led intervention in 1979, and the country returned to a presidential system of government. Since then, the Central African Republic has experienced periods of relative stability and instability, with various governments struggling to maintain control over the country's diverse ethnic groups and vast natural resources.

text: Alphabets are usually associated with a standard ordering of letters. This makes them useful for purposes of collation, which allows words to be sorted in a specific order, commonly known as the alphabetical order. It also means that their letters can be used as an alternative method of "numbering" ordered items, in such contexts as numbered lists and number placements. There are also names for letters in some languages. This is known as acrophony; It is present in some modern scripts, such as Greek, and many Semitic scripts, such as Arabic, Hebrew, and Syriac. It was used in some ancient alphabets, such as in Phoenician. However, this system is not present in all languages, such as the Latin alphabet, which adds a vowel after a character for each letter. Some systems also used to have this system but later on abandoned it for a system similar to Latin, such as Cyrillic.

text: The Brittonic languages are a branch of the Celtic languages that were spoken in Great Britain from pre-Roman times until the Middle English period. They are also known as Brythonic or P-Celtic languages, and include Welsh, Breton, and Cornish, among others. The term "Brittonic" is derived from the Latin name for the Britons, a Celtic-speaking people who inhabited Great Britain before the Roman conquest in the 1st century AD. The Brittonic languages are thought to have evolved from a common ancestor with the Goidelic languages, which were spoken in Ireland and Scotland. Over time, the Brittonic languages developed distinct grammatical and phonological features, such as initial consonant mutation and a system of vowel harmony. Despite their decline in the Middle English period, the Brittonic languages have experienced revivals in recent centuries, with Cornish being officially recognized as a minority language in the UK and Welsh remaining an official language in Wales.

text: On 7 December 1941, a group of 12 B-17s of the 38th (four B-17C) and 88th (eight B-17E) Reconnaissance Squadrons, en route to reinforce the Philippines, was flown into Pearl Harbor from Hamilton Field, California, arriving while the surprise attack on Pearl Harbor was going on. Leonard "Smitty" Smith Humiston, co-pilot on First Lieutenant Robert H. Richards' B-17C, AAF S/N 40-2049, reported that he thought the U.S. Navy was giving the flight a 21-gun salute to celebrate the arrival of the bombers, after which he realized that Pearl Harbor was under attack. The Fortress came under fire from Japanese fighter aircraft, though the crew was unharmed with the exception of one member who suffered an abrasion on his hand. Japanese activity forced them to divert from Hickam Field to Bellows Field. On landing, the aircraft overran the runway and ran into a ditch, where it was then strafed. Although initially deemed repairable, 40-2049 (11th BG / 38th RS) received more than 200 bullet holes and never flew again. Ten of the 12 Fortresses survived the attack.

text: Anxiety is a common mental health condition characterized by feelings of worry, nervousness, and fear that are persistent and overwhelming. In some cases, anxiety can become a debilitating condition that interferes with daily life, relationships, and work performance. Research suggests that anxiety is often linked to underlying factors such as genetics, brain chemistry, and life experiences, although the exact causes are still not fully understood. text: A colloid is a mixture in which one substance is divided into minute particles that are dispersed throughout another substance. Colloids can be formed through various methods, including mechanical dispersion, chemical synthesis, or biological processes. Examples of colloids include milk, where fat molecules are dispersed in water, and ink, where pigment particles are suspended in a liquid carrier. The properties of colloids make them useful in a wide range of applications, from food and cosmetics to pharmaceuticals and materials science.

Following Al-Wahid's death, his descendants continued to play a significant role in regional politics, often serving as governors or military leaders under different dynasties. In some historical accounts, the term "Wahidi" is also used more broadly to describe the people of the region of Oman and surrounding areas during the early Islamic period. The Wahidi family's influence extended into the medieval period, with some members holding prominent positions in trade and commerce. However, their power and influence eventually waned as other regional powers rose to prominence. Today, the term "Wahidi" is primarily used by historians and scholars to refer to this specific branch of the Banu Asad tribe.

text: Tables games first appeared in France during the 11th century and became a favourite pastime of gamblers. In 1254, Louis IX issued a decree prohibiting his court officials and subjects from playing. They were played in Germany in the 12th century, and had reached Iceland by the 13th century. In Spain, the Alfonso X manuscript Libro de los juegos, completed in 1283, describes rules for a number of dice and table games in addition to its discussion of chess. By the 17th century, games at tables had spread to Sweden. A wooden board and counters were recovered from the wreck of the Vasa among the belongings of the ship's officers. Tables games appear widely in paintings of this period, mainly those of Dutch and German painters, such as Van Ostade, Jan Steen, Hieronymus Bosch, and Bruegel. Among surviving artworks are Cardsharps by Caravaggio.

text: Tolerance The main problem of the chronic use of benzodiazepines is the development of tolerance and dependence. Tolerance manifests itself as diminished pharmacological effect and develops relatively quickly to the sedative, hypnotic, anticonvulsant, and muscle relaxant actions of benzodiazepines. Tolerance to anti-anxiety effects develops more slowly with little evidence of continued effectiveness beyond four to six months of continued use. In general, tolerance to the amnesic effects does not occur. However, controversy exists as to tolerance to the anxiolytic effects with some evidence that benzodiazepines retain efficacy and opposing evidence from a systematic review of the literature that tolerance frequently occurs and some evidence that anxiety may worsen with long-term use. The question of tolerance to the amnesic effects of benzodiazepines is, likewise, unclear. Some evidence suggests that partial tolerance does develop, and that, "memory impairment is limited to a narrow window within 90 minutes after each dose".

text: Confucianism is a philosophical and ethical system that originated in ancient China. It is based on the teachings of Confucius, a Chinese philosopher who lived from 551 to 479 BCE. The core principles of Confucianism emphasize personal and governmental morality, correctness of social relationships, justice, and sincerity. At its heart, Confucianism focuses on cultivating virtues such as ren (benevolence), yi (righteousness), and li (etiquette) through self-cultivation and education. The system also emphasizes the importance of social hierarchy and respect for authority, with a strong emphasis on filial piety towards parents and ancestors. Confucianism has had a profound impact on East Asian cultures, influencing not only China but also Japan, Korea, and Vietnam. Over time, it has evolved to incorporate elements from Buddhism, Taoism, and other philosophical traditions, resulting in diverse interpretations and practices across different regions and communities. Despite its evolution, the fundamental principles of Confucianism remain a cornerstone of traditional East Asian thought and continue to shape social norms and values in many parts of Asia today.

text: Avicenna was first schooled in the Quran and literature, and by the age of 10, he had memorized the entire Quran. Some time later, Avicenna's father invited the physician and philosopher Abu Abdallah al-Natili to their house to educate Avicenna. Together, they studied the Isagoge of Porphyry (died 305) and possibly the Categories of Aristotle (died 322 BC) as well. After Avicenna had read the Almagest of Ptolemy (died 170) and Euclid's Elements, Natili told him to continue his research independently. By the time Avicenna was eighteen, he was well-educated in Greek sciences. Although Avicenna only mentions Natili as his teacher in his autobiography, he most likely had other teachers as well, such as the physicians Abu Mansur Qumri and Abu Sahl al-Masihi.

text: The Articles of Confederation was the first constitution of the United States, adopted by the Continental Congress on November 15, 1777. It served as a framework for the newly independent states to govern themselves and interact with one another until it was replaced by the United States Constitution in 1789. The document established a loose alliance between the states, with each state retaining its sovereignty and independence. A unicameral Congress, comprising delegates from each state, was given limited powers to make laws, declare war, and regulate foreign affairs. However, the Articles also imposed significant constraints on the federal government's authority, requiring unanimous consent among the states for most decisions. This led to difficulties in implementing policies and resolving disputes between states, ultimately contributing to the need for a more robust system of governance. Despite its limitations, the Articles played an important role in shaping the early years of American history and laid the groundwork for the development of the federal government under the Constitution.

text: The Alien and Sedition Acts were passed by Congress while it was controlled by the Federalist Party in 1798. Members of the Federalist Party grew increasingly distrustful of the opposing Democratic-Republican Party with the Democratic-Republicans' support of France in the midst of the French Revolution. Some appeared to desire a similar revolution in the United States to overthrow the government and social structure. Newspapers sympathizing with each side exacerbated the tensions by accusing the other side's leaders of corruption, incompetence, and treason. The spreading unrest in Europe and calls for secession in the United States appeared to threaten the newly formed American republic. Some of this agitation was seen by Federalists as having been caused by French and French-sympathizing immigrants. The Alien and Sedition Acts were supported for different reasons, including the prevention of potential unrest by targeting immigrants, restriction of speech that may induce crime, reduction of partisan divides by penalizing expressly partisan speech, and suppression of political opponents of the Federalists.

text: The churching of women is a Christian practice that involves the ritualistic purification and readmission to worship for women who have given birth. During this time, women were often expected to abstain from certain activities, such as bathing or participating in social events, and may have been required to make offerings or perform specific rituals to purify themselves. The churching ceremony itself usually took place when the woman was deemed sufficiently purified, and involved a ritual of prayer, anointing with oil, and sometimes the reading of scripture. This practice has largely fallen out of use in modern times, although some Christian denominations may still observe it as a way to mark the transition from postpartum isolation back into community life.

text: Hatafat dam brit A brit milah is more than circumcision; it is a sacred ritual in Judaism, as distinguished from its non-ritual requirement in Islam. One ramification is that the brit is not considered complete unless a drop of blood is actually drawn. The standard medical methods of circumcision through constriction do not meet the requirements of the halakhah for brit milah, because they are done with hemostasis, i.e., they stop the flow of blood. Moreover, circumcision alone, in the absence of the brit milah ceremony, does not fulfill the requirements of the mitzvah. Therefore, in cases involving a Jew who was circumcised outside of a brit milah, an already-circumcised convert, or an aposthetic (born without a foreskin) individual, the mohel draws a symbolic drop of blood (, ) from the penis at the point where the foreskin would have been or was attached.

Anabaptism also placed a strong emphasis on pacifism, nonconformity to worldly authority, and separation from the state. As a result, many Anabaptists faced persecution and were forced to live in secret or migrate to other areas where they could practice their faith freely. Before, L. limborgi was assumed to have direct development (eggs hatching as tiny, full-formed frogs), but more careful observations have showed it has free-swimming but endotrophic larvae; this probably applies to the closely related L. hascheanus, too. text: Human reproduction is the biological process by which humans produce offspring. It involves the fusion of male and female gametes, or sex cells, resulting in fertilization and subsequent development into a fetus. The reproductive system in both males and females consists of organs and tissues that work together to facilitate this process. In females, the ovaries produce ova, or eggs, which are released from the ovaries through ovulation and travel down the fallopian tubes towards the uterus. Meanwhile, in males, the testes produce sperm, which are stored in the epididymis and transported through the vas deferens to the urethra for ejaculation. When a male's sperm meets a female's egg during intercourse or other forms of assisted reproduction, fertilization occurs, resulting in the formation of a zygote. The zygote then undergoes several cell divisions and develops into an embryo, which eventually implants in the uterus lining and begins to grow into a fetus. This process typically takes around nine months to complete, culminating in childbirth and the birth of a new individual.

text: NTLMv2 sends two responses to an 8-byte server challenge. Each response contains a 16-byte HMAC-MD5 hash of the server challenge, a fully/partially randomly generated client challenge, and an HMAC-MD5 hash of the user's password and other identifying information. The two responses differ in the format of the client challenge. The shorter response uses an 8-byte random value for this challenge. In order to verify the response, the server must receive as part of the response the client challenge. For this shorter response, the 8-byte client challenge appended to the 16-byte response makes a 24-byte package which is consistent with the 24-byte response format of the previous NTLMv1 protocol. In certain non-official documentation (e.g. DCE/RPC Over SMB, Leighton) this response is termed LMv2.

text: A zero-suppressed decision diagram is a type of data structure used in computer science to represent and manipulate Boolean functions. It is an extension of the binary decision diagram (BDD) that suppresses redundant nodes by eliminating those with a value of 0, thereby reducing the size of the diagram. This compression technique allows for more efficient storage and manipulation of large Boolean functions, which are commonly encountered in digital circuit design, model checking, and other areas of computer-aided design. The zero-suppressed decision diagram is constructed similarly to a BDD, with each node representing a variable or its complement, but it includes an additional flag indicating whether the node's value is 0. This flag enables the elimination of nodes that are guaranteed to have a value of 0, resulting in a more compact representation of the Boolean function. The zero-suppressed decision diagram has applications in various fields where efficient manipulation and storage of large Boolean functions are crucial.

The development of abzymes has also led to the creation of novel therapeutic agents, which are being explored for their potential to treat various diseases, including cancer and autoimmune disorders. By modifying these cognitive distortions, individuals can develop more adaptive coping strategies and improve their emotional regulation. Cognitive behavioral therapy is often used to treat a range of mental health conditions, including anxiety disorders, depression, post-traumatic stress disorder, and substance use disorders. The therapy typically involves a combination of techniques, such as cognitive restructuring, exposure, and problem-solving, which are tailored to the individual's specific needs and goals. text: In ethical philosophy, consequentialism is a class of normative, teleological ethical theories that holds that the consequences of one's conduct are the ultimate basis for judgement about the rightness or wrongness of that conduct. Thus, from a consequentialist standpoint, a morally right act (or omission from acting) is one that will produce a good outcome. Consequentialism, along with eudaimonism, falls under the broader category of teleological ethics, a group of views which claim that the moral value of any act consists in its tendency to produce things of intrinsic value. Consequentialists hold in general that an act is right if and only if the act (or in some views, the rule under which it falls) will produce, will probably produce, or is intended to produce, a greater balance of good over evil than any available alternative. Different consequentialist theories differ in how they define moral goods, with chief candidates including pleasure, the absence of pain, the satisfaction of one's preferences, and broader notions of the "general good".

text: Augustine Schulte described the ceremony in the early twentieth century: The mother, kneels in the vestibule, or within the church, carrying a lighted candle. The priest, vested in surplice and white stole, sprinkles her with holy water in the form of a cross. Having recited Psalm 24, "The earth is the Lord's and the fullness thereof", he offers her the left extremity of the stole and leads her into the church, saying: "Enter thou into the temple of God, adore the Son of the Blessed Virgin Mary who has given thee fruitfulness of offspring." She advances to one of the altars and kneels before it, whilst the priest, turned towards her, recites the appropriate blessing, and then, having sprinkled her again with holy water in the form of the cross, dismisses her, saying: "The peace and blessing of God Almighty, the Father, the Son, and the Holy Ghost, descend upon thee, and remain forever. According to commentary provided in a modern Catholic tutorial on the mass, the fact that the priest goes to meet the mother and escort her into the church is in itself a mark of respect for her.

Chinese popular or folk religion, which is framed by the three teachings and other traditions, consists in allegiance to the shen (), a character that signifies the "energies of generation", who can be deities of the environment or ancestral principles of human groups, concepts of civility, culture heroes, many of whom feature in Chinese mythology and history. Among the most popular cults are those of Mazu (goddess of the seas), Huangdi (one of the two divine patriarchs of the Chinese race), Guandi (god of war and business), Caishen (god of prosperity and richness), Pangu and many others. text: The swing in Australian politics refers to the change in a party's or candidate's two-party preferred vote share between an election and its preceding one. A positive swing indicates that a party or candidate has gained support, while a negative swing suggests they have lost ground. Swings can be significant in determining the outcome of elections and are often closely watched by analysts and commentators. In Australian federal elections, swings are usually reported at the national level as well as for individual electorates. The size and direction of the swing can vary significantly across different regions and demographics, providing insights into voter preferences and trends. Overall, the concept of a swing is an important aspect of understanding electoral dynamics in Australia.

Through this device, Skinner demonstrated that behavior can be modified by its consequences, such as rewards or punishments, leading to the concept of operant conditioning. This theory posits that behavior is shaped by external factors and can be influenced through reinforcement, punishment, or extinction. text: Systems can be designed to use passive cooling alone, reducing noise and eliminating moving parts that may fail. This can be achieved by: Natural convection cooling: carefully designed, correctly oriented, and sufficiently large heatsinks can dissipate up to 100 W by natural convection alone Heatpipes to transfer heat out of the case Undervolting or underclocking to reduce power dissipation Submersive liquid cooling, placing the motherboard in a non-electrically conductive fluid, provides excellent convection cooling and protects from humidity and water without the need for heatsinks or fans. Special care must be taken to ensure compatibility with adhesives and sealants used on the motherboard and ICs. This solution is used in some external environments such as wireless equipment located in the wild. Other methods of cooling include: Water cooling Mineral oil Liquid nitrogen Refrigeration, e.g. by Peltier effect devices Ionic wind cooling is being researched, whereby air is moved by ionizing air between two electrodes. This replaces the fan and has the advantage of no moving parts and less noise.

text: The Brouwer fixed-point theorem is a fundamental result in topology that states that any continuous function from a closed n-dimensional ball to itself has at least one fixed point. In other words, if f is a continuous function mapping the interior of an n-dimensional sphere onto its surface and then back into the sphere, there exists a point within the sphere that remains unchanged under this transformation. This theorem was first proved by Dutch mathematician L.E.J. Brouwer in 1910 as part of his work on topology and has since become a cornerstone of the field. The theorem's implications are far-reaching, with applications in various areas such as differential equations, dynamical systems, and game theory. It is also used to establish the existence of solutions to certain types of optimization problems and to provide insights into the behavior of complex systems. The Brouwer fixed-point theorem has been generalized and extended by other mathematicians over the years, but its core idea remains a fundamental concept in topology and mathematics.

This interaction results in a complex circulation pattern that influences ocean currents and climate patterns globally. The Antarctic Circumpolar Current also has a significant impact on marine ecosystems, supporting a diverse range of marine life, including krill, penguins, and whales. text: Dmitrii Mendeleev noticed that when he arranged the elements in a row according to their atomic weights, there was a certain periodicity to them. For instance, the second element, lithium, had similar properties to the ninth element, sodium, and the sixteenth element, potassium — a period of seven. Likewise, beryllium, magnesium, and calcium were similar and all were seven places apart from each other on Mendeleev's table (eight places apart on the modern table). Using these patterns, Mendeleev predicted the existence and properties of new elements, which were later discovered in nature: scandium, gallium, and germanium. Moreover, the periodic table could predict how many atoms of other elements that an atom could bond with — e.g., germanium and carbon are in the same group on the table and their atoms both combine with two oxygen atoms each (GeO2 and CO2). Mendeleev found these patterns to confirm the hypothesis that matter is made of atoms because it showed that the elements could be categorized by their atomic weight. Inserting a new element into the middle of a period would break the parallel between that period and the next, and would also violate Dalton's law of multiple proportions.

text: Analysis of variance is a statistical technique used to compare the means of two or more groups to determine if there are any significant differences between them. It involves partitioning the total variation in a dataset into different components that can be attributed to specific sources, such as group membership or experimental treatments. The analysis of variance (ANOVA) model assumes that the data follow a normal distribution and that the variances of the groups being compared are equal. If these assumptions are met, ANOVA can provide an F-statistic, which is used to determine whether the observed differences between the groups are statistically significant. A large F-statistic indicates that the differences between the groups are unlikely to be due to chance, suggesting that there may be a real effect of the independent variable on the dependent variable. The results of ANOVA can be used to identify which groups differ significantly from one another and to estimate the magnitude of these effects.

text: The earliest and most widely known scientific theory of antidepressant action is the monoamine hypothesis, which can be traced back to the 1950s and 1960s. This theory states that depression is due to an imbalance, most often a deficiency, of the monoamine neurotransmitters, namely serotonin, norepinephrine, and/or dopamine. However, serotonin in particular has been implicated, as in the serotonin hypothesis of depression. The monoamine hypothesis was originally proposed based on observations that reserpine, a drug which depletes the monoamine neurotransmitters, produced depressive effects in people, and that certain hydrazine antituberculosis agents like iproniazid, which prevent the breakdown of monoamine neurotransmitters, produced apparent antidepressant effects. Most currently marketed antidepressants, which are monoaminergic in their actions, are theoretically consistent with the monoamine hypothesis. Despite the widespread nature of the monoamine hypothesis, it has a number of limitations: for one, all monoaminergic antidepressants have a delayed onset of action of at least a week; and secondly, many people with depression do not respond to monoaminergic antidepressants. A number of alternative hypotheses have been proposed, including hypotheses involving glutamate, neurogenesis, epigenetics, cortisol hypersecretion, and inflammation, among others.

text: An antigen is a substance that can induce an immune response in an organism. It is typically a protein or polysaccharide molecule that the body recognizes as foreign and mounts an immune reaction against it. The immune system produces antibodies that bind specifically to the antigen, marking it for destruction. Antigens can also be self-antigens, which are normal components of the body's own tissues that may become exposed due to injury or disease, triggering an autoimmune response. In some cases, antigens can be used as vaccines to stimulate a protective immune response against specific diseases. The interaction between antigens and the immune system is complex and highly regulated, involving multiple cell types and signaling pathways.

text: Consequentialism is a moral and ethical theory that emphasizes the importance of consequences or outcomes in determining the rightness or wrongness of an action. The priest (Rabbi) then removes a ring made of myrtle worn by the baptized and places it on their forehead. This is then followed by a handshake (kushta, "hand of truth") with the priest. The final blessing involves the priest laying his right hand on the baptized person's head. text: Active Directory is a directory service developed by Microsoft for Windows domain networks. In those days I was just getting into contrapuntal and choral writing...and I wanted to develop my technique by writing a trio. What I didn't want to do is the quodlibet method...wouldn't it be nice to have three songs you don't think are going to go together, and they do go together ... The trick was the little vamp on "Soon" which has five-and six-note chords." While some conspiracy theories may have a basis in fact or raise legitimate concerns about government actions or corporate practices, many others are unfounded and lack empirical evidence to support them. text: The Accelerated Graphics Port, commonly abbreviated as AGP, is a high-speed interface standard for connecting and communicating between a computer's motherboard and its graphics card. The AGP slot on the motherboard allows for direct connection of the graphics card, bypassing the need for a separate expansion card or bridge chip. This results in improved performance and reduced latency for graphics-intensive applications such as gaming and video editing. AGP slots were typically 4x, 8x, or 16x, indicating their maximum bandwidth capabilities, with higher speeds allowing for more complex graphics processing. Salinger's The Catcher in the Rye is a well-known modern example of fictional autobiography. Charlotte Brontë's Jane Eyre is yet another example of fictional autobiography, as noted on the front page of the original version. text: Counterpoint is a musical technique used to create multiple independent melodies that interweave and blend together in harmony. In counterpoint, the individual melodies are not simply layered on top of one another, but rather they interact and respond to each other in a way that creates a sense of tension and release. This can be achieved through various techniques such as imitation, where one melody imitates the shape or contour of another, or inversion, where one melody is inverted or reversed in relation to another. text: Background and context The political push to increase cooperation among the then-loyal colonies began with the Albany Congress in 1754 and Benjamin Franklin's proposed Albany Plan, an inter-colonial collaboration to help solve mutual local problems. Over the next two decades, some of the basic concepts it addressed would strengthen; others would weaken, especially in the degree of loyalty (or lack thereof) owed the Crown. Civil disobedience resulted in coercive and quelling measures, such as the passage of what the colonials referred to as the Intolerable Acts in the British Parliament, and armed skirmishes which resulted in dissidents being proclaimed rebels. These actions eroded the number of Crown Loyalists (Tories) among the colonials and, together with the highly effective propaganda campaign of the Patriot leaders, caused an increasing number of colonists to begin agitating for independence from the mother country. In 1775, with events outpacing communications, the Second Continental Congress began acting as the provisional government for the United Colonies.
label: human

text: According to the Life, Pepin died in Paris on 24 September 768, whereupon the kingship passed jointly to his sons, "with divine assent" (divino nutu). The Franks "in general assembly" (generali conventu) gave them both the rank of a king (reges) but "partitioned the whole body of the kingdom equally" (totum regni corpus ex aequo partirentur). The annals tell a slightly different version, with the king dying at St-Denis, near Paris. The two "lords" (domni) were "elevated to kingship" (elevati sunt in regnum), Charles on 9 October in Noyon, Carloman on an unspecified date in Soissons. If born in 742, Charles was 26 years old, but he had been campaigning at his father's right hand for several years, which may help to account for his military skill. Carloman was 17.
