text: Barter is a form of exchange where individuals or groups trade goods or services without using a medium of exchange such as money. This practice has been observed in various cultures throughout history and continues to exist today in some parts of the world. In bartering, two parties agree to exchange one item for another, with the value of each item being determined by mutual agreement. Barter can be used to acquire goods or services that are not readily available through other means, such as during times of economic hardship or when access to traditional markets is limited. It can also be used to reduce reliance on monetary systems and promote self-sufficiency. However, bartering can be complex and time-consuming, requiring a high degree of coordination between parties to ensure that the exchange is mutually beneficial. As a result, it is often used in conjunction with other forms of exchange or as a supplement to traditional economic activities.
label: llm

text: The Basque language, also known as Euskara, is a unique and ancient language spoken in the Basque Country, an autonomous region located in northern Spain and southwestern France. It is not part of any known language family and is considered a linguistic isolate, meaning that it does not share similarities with any other language. The origins of Basque are unclear, but it is believed to have been spoken in the region for thousands of years. There are two main dialects of Basque: Batua, which is standardized and widely used in education and media, and Zeramika, a more informal dialect spoken by many native speakers. Despite its isolation, Basque has had significant cultural and linguistic influence on the surrounding regions, particularly in the Pyrenees mountains where it shares borders with France and Spain. Today, there are approximately 600,000 people who speak Basque as their first or second language, making it a vital part of the region's identity and heritage.

text: Continuum mechanics is a branch of physics and engineering that deals with the study of the behavior of materials under various types of loading conditions. It assumes that the material can be treated as a continuous medium, rather than being composed of individual particles or atoms. This approach allows for the use of mathematical models to describe the deformation and flow of materials in response to external forces, such as tension, compression, shear, and torsion. The fundamental principles of continuum mechanics include the conservation of mass, momentum, and energy, which are used to derive equations that govern the behavior of materials under different loading conditions. These equations can be applied to a wide range of problems, including the analysis of stress and strain in solids, the flow of fluids through pipes and channels, and the vibration and stability of structures. By using continuum mechanics, engineers and physicists can design and optimize systems, predict their performance, and understand the underlying mechanisms that govern their behavior.

He regained consciousness and started to improve, but contracted bronchopneumonia. He suffered a cardiac arrest on January 22, and on January 25, surrounded by his family in his home, died after his heart failed as a result of apoplexy. His body was transported back to Chicago a week later and a private funeral was held. He was originally buried at Mount Olivet Cemetery in Chicago. In 1950, Capone's remains, along with those of his father, Gabriele, and brother, Frank, were moved to Mount Carmel Cemetery in Hillside, Illinois.

The series explores themes of survival, power struggles, and the human condition in the face of catastrophic loss. Each novel is a standalone story, but they are all connected by their shared universe and events that unfold throughout the series. The Darkness series has been praised for its gritty realism, complex characters, and thought-provoking exploration of what it means to be human in the face of overwhelming adversity. text: Aldebaran is a star located in the constellation Taurus, approximately 65 light-years from Earth. It is one of the brightest stars in the night sky and is easily visible to the naked eye due to its relatively close proximity and high luminosity. Aldebaran is an orange giant star, classified as a K5 IIIa spectral type, indicating that it has exhausted its hydrogen fuel and expanded into a larger size. The star's surface temperature is around 3,800 Kelvin, which is cooler than the Sun's surface temperature of approximately 5,500 Kelvin. Aldebaran is also known for its relatively high mass loss rate, with estimates suggesting that it loses about one solar mass every billion years due to stellar wind and other processes. The star's name, Aldebaran, is derived from the Arabic phrase "al-dabaran," meaning "the follower" or "the guide," likely referring to its position as a navigational aid in ancient astronomy.

text: Black ink, invented in China, was traditionally used in the Middle Ages for writing, for the simple reason that black was the darkest color and therefore provided the greatest contrast with white paper or parchment, making it the easiest color to read. It became even more important in the 15th century, with the invention of printing. A new kind of ink, printer's ink, was created out of soot, turpentine and walnut oil. The new ink made it possible to spread ideas to a mass audience through printed books, and to popularize art through black and white engravings and prints. Because of its contrast and clarity, black ink on white paper continued to be the standard for printing books, newspapers and documents; and for the same reason black text on a white background is the most common format used on computer screens.

text: Education From 1832, when she was seventeen, her mathematical abilities began to emerge, and her interest in mathematics dominated the majority of her adult life. Her mother's obsession with rooting out any of the insanity of which she accused Byron was one of the reasons that Ada was taught mathematics from an early age. She was privately educated in mathematics and science by William Frend, William King, and Mary Somerville, the noted 19th-century researcher and scientific author. In the 1840s, the mathematician Augustus De Morgan extended her "much help in her mathematical studies" including study of advanced calculus topics including the "numbers of Bernoulli" (that formed her celebrated algorithm for Babbage's Analytical Engine). In a letter to Lady Byron, De Morgan suggested that Ada's skill in mathematics might lead her to become "an original mathematical investigator, perhaps of first-rate eminence".

The biggest advantage is it frees in-house resources from staffing and managing complex computer projects. Many application service providers target small, fast-growing companies with limited information system resources. On the other hand, larger companies with major systems will likely have their technical infrastructure in place. One risk is having to trust an external organization with sensitive information. Another risk is having to trust the provider's infrastructure reliability.

text: The Afroasiatic language family is a proposed language family that encompasses a diverse range of languages spoken across the Middle East, North Africa, and parts of the Horn of Africa. The family includes languages such as Arabic, Amharic, Hebrew, Swahili, and Hausa, among others. These languages are not necessarily closely related in terms of grammar or vocabulary, but they share certain phonological and morphological features that have led some linguists to propose a common ancestral language. The Afroasiatic family is often divided into several branches, including Semitic, Berber, Chadic, Cushitic, and Omotic, each with its own distinct characteristics. Despite the lack of conclusive evidence for a single ancestral language, the similarities between these languages have led many scholars to accept Afroasiatic as a valid language family. The study of Afroasiatic languages has shed light on the linguistic history of the region and has provided insights into the migration patterns and cultural exchange that have shaped the languages spoken in this area over time.

text: No Apatosaurus skull was mentioned in literature until the 1970s when John Stanton McIntosh and David Berman redescribed the skulls of Diplodocus and Apatosaurus. They found that though he never published his opinion, Holland was almost certainly correct, that Apatosaurus had a Diplodocus-like skull. According to them, many skulls long thought to pertain to Diplodocus might instead be those of Apatosaurus. They reassigned multiple skulls to Apatosaurus based on associated and closely associated vertebrae. Even though they supported Holland, it was noted that Apatosaurus might have possessed a Camarasaurus-like skull, based on a disarticulated Camarasaurus-like tooth found at the precise site where an Apatosaurus specimen was found years before. On October20, 1979, after the publications by McIntosh and Berman, the first true skull of Apatosaurus was mounted on a skeleton in a museum, that of the Carnegie. In 1998, it was suggested that the Felch Quarry skull that Marsh had included in his 1896 skeletal restoration instead belonged to Brachiosaurus.

text: Botanists study how plants produce food and how to increase yields, for example through plant breeding, making their work important to humanity's ability to feed the world and provide food security for future generations. Botanists also study weeds, which are a considerable problem in agriculture, and the biology and control of plant pathogens in agriculture and natural ecosystems. Ethnobotany is the study of the relationships between plants and people. When applied to the investigation of historical plant–people relationships ethnobotany may be referred to as archaeobotany or palaeoethnobotany. Some of the earliest plant-people relationships arose between the indigenous people of Canada in identifying edible plants from inedible plants. This relationship the indigenous people had with plants was recorded by ethnobotanists.

text: The history of baseball in the United States dates back to the mid-19th century. The modern game is believed to have originated from various bat-and-ball games played in England and Ireland, which were brought over by immigrants. The first organized game was played in 1846 in Hoboken, New Jersey, with Alexander Cartwright credited as a key figure in developing the rules of the game. The National Association of Base Ball Players (NABBP) was formed in 1857 to standardize the rules and promote the sport. As the game gained popularity, professional teams emerged, including the Cincinnati Red Stockings, who are considered one of the first all-professional baseball teams. In 1876, the National League of Professional Baseball Clubs was established, marking the beginning of organized professional baseball in the United States. The game continued to grow and evolve throughout the late 19th and early 20th centuries, with the introduction of the World Series in 1903 and the establishment of Major League Baseball (MLB) as we know it today.

text: If the interaction energy is greater than kT, the attractive forces will prevail, and the colloidal particles will begin to clump together. This process is referred to generally as aggregation, but is also referred to as flocculation, coagulation or precipitation. While these terms are often used interchangeably, for some definitions they have slightly different meanings. For example, coagulation can be used to describe irreversible, permanent aggregation where the forces holding the particles together are stronger than any external forces caused by stirring or mixing. Flocculation can be used to describe reversible aggregation involving weaker attractive forces, and the aggregate is usually called a floc. The term precipitation is normally reserved for describing a phase change from a colloid dispersion to a solid (precipitate) when it is subjected to a perturbation. Aggregation causes sedimentation or creaming, therefore the colloid is unstable: if either of these processes occur the colloid will no longer be a suspension.

text: The Battle of Gettysburg was a pivotal engagement fought during the American Civil War from July 1 to July 3, 1863. The battle took place in and around the town of Gettysburg, Pennsylvania, and involved Union forces under General George Meade and Confederate forces led by General Robert E. Lee. The battle began on July 1 when Confederate cavalry encountered Union infantry near Gettysburg, leading to a series of intense battles over the next three days. On July 2, Lee's army launched a massive assault against the Union positions on Cemetery Ridge, but was repelled with heavy casualties. The following day, Lee attempted another assault, known as Pickett's Charge, which was also unsuccessful. On July 3, the Confederates retreated from Gettysburg, marking a decisive Union victory. The battle is considered a turning point in the war, as it halted Lee's invasion of the North and set the stage for the ultimate defeat of the Confederate States of America.

text: The butterfly effect is a concept in chaos theory that describes how small, seemingly insignificant events can have a significant impact on a larger system. The term was coined by American meteorologist Edward Lorenz, who discovered that even the tiniest variation in atmospheric conditions could drastically alter weather patterns. This idea suggests that the flapping of a butterfly's wings could potentially cause a hurricane on the other side of the world. While this may seem far-fetched, it illustrates the inherent unpredictability and sensitivity to initial conditions that characterizes complex systems. In essence, the butterfly effect implies that even the smallest perturbation can cascade into a larger phenomenon, making long-term predictions increasingly difficult. This concept has been applied to various fields beyond meteorology, including physics, biology, economics, and social sciences, where it is used to describe the potential for small events to have significant effects on complex systems.

text: Prescription mouthwashes are used prior to and after oral surgery procedures, such as tooth extraction, or to treat the pain associated with mucositis caused by radiation therapy or chemotherapy. They are also prescribed for aphthous ulcers, other oral ulcers, and other mouth pain. "Magic mouthwashes" are prescription mouthwashes compounded in a pharmacy from a list of ingredients specified by a doctor. Despite a lack of evidence that prescription mouthwashes are more effective in decreasing the pain of oral lesions, many patients and prescribers continue to use them. There has been only one controlled study to evaluate the efficacy of magic mouthwash; it shows no difference in efficacy between the most common magic-mouthwash formulation, on the one hand, and commercial mouthwashes (such as chlorhexidine) or a saline/baking soda solution, on the other. Current guidelines suggest that saline solution is just as effective as magic mouthwash in pain relief and in shortening the healing time of oral mucositis from cancer therapies.

text: British Rail was the national railway company of the United Kingdom from 1948 to 1997. It was formed by the merger of several private railway companies and took over the majority of the country's rail network. British Rail's operations were overseen by the British Transport Commission, which was responsible for setting policy and overseeing the company's finances. During its existence, British Rail introduced several notable innovations, including the InterCity 125 high-speed train and the Class 91 electric locomotive. text: An antioxidant is a substance that prevents or slows down the oxidation of other molecules. Oxidation is a chemical reaction that involves the loss of electrons, which can lead to cell damage and potentially cause harm to living organisms. Antioxidants are found in various forms, including vitamins C and E, beta-carotene, and polyphenols, among others. They work by neutralizing free radicals, which are unstable molecules that contain unpaired electrons and can initiate oxidation reactions. By stabilizing these free radicals, antioxidants prevent the chain reaction of oxidation that can lead to cell damage. Antioxidants play a crucial role in maintaining cellular health and have been linked to various benefits, including reducing inflammation, improving cardiovascular health, and potentially lowering the risk of certain diseases. They are also found naturally in many foods, such as fruits, vegetables, nuts, and seeds, and can be obtained through dietary supplements or fortified products.

Having been abandoned by both his supporters and his father-in-law, Alexios V was captured near Mosynopolis, or possibly in Anatolia, by the advancing Latins under Thierry de Loos in November 1204. On his return to Constantinople as a prisoner, Alexios V was tried for treason against Alexios IV. In his trial the blind ex-emperor argued that it was Alexios IV who had committed treason to his country, through his intention to invite the crusaders to enter Constantinople in force. text: A beta sheet is a type of secondary structure found in proteins. It consists of two or more polypeptide chains that are twisted together in a pleated sheet-like arrangement. The chains are held together by hydrogen bonds between the carbonyl oxygen atoms and the amide hydrogen atoms of adjacent amino acids, forming a stable and compact structure. Beta sheets can be classified into different types based on their orientation, including parallel beta sheets where the strands run in the same direction and antiparallel beta sheets where the strands run in opposite directions. The arrangement of beta sheets within a protein can vary greatly, with some proteins containing multiple layers or even irregularly shaped structures. In general, beta sheets are stabilized by hydrophobic interactions between non-polar amino acids on the surface of the sheet, which helps to maintain their structure and contribute to the overall stability of the protein. The presence and arrangement of beta sheets can have significant effects on a protein's function and activity.

text: The metopes of the Parthenon are a series of rectangular panels that adorn the exterior of the temple's frieze. Located on the east and west sides of the building, these metopes depict scenes from Greek mythology and everyday life. The northern and southern sides display scenes of Theseus battling the Minotaur and the Labors of Hercules, respectively. Each metope is carved with intricate detail and precision, showcasing the skill and craftsmanship of ancient Greek sculptors. The metopes are an integral part of the Parthenon's architectural design, serving as a visual representation of the temple's themes and ideals. They were likely created by a team of skilled artisans working under the supervision of Phidias, the renowned Athenian sculptor who designed the Parthenon's sculptures.

Islam is the predominant religion in Afghanistan, with approximately 99% of the population identifying as Muslim. Afghans are known for their hospitality and warm welcome to guests, which is deeply rooted in their cultural traditions. text: The Aramaic alphabet is historically significant since virtually all modern Middle Eastern writing systems can be traced back to it. That is primarily due to the widespread usage of the Aramaic language after it was adopted as both a lingua franca and the official language of the Neo-Assyrian and Neo-Babylonian Empires, and their successor, the Achaemenid Empire. Among the descendant scripts in modern use, the Jewish Hebrew alphabet bears the closest relation to the Imperial Aramaic script of the 5th century BC, with an identical letter inventory and, for the most part, nearly identical letter shapes. By contrast the Samaritan Hebrew script is directly descended from Proto-Hebrew/Phoenician script, which was in turn the ancestor of the Aramaic alphabet. The Aramaic alphabet was also an ancestor to the Nabataean alphabet, which in turn had the Arabic alphabet as a descendant.

text: Video game culture is a worldwide new media subculture formed around video games and game playing. As computer and video games have increased in popularity over time, they have had a significant influence on popular culture. Video game culture has also evolved over time hand in hand with internet culture as well as the increasing popularity of mobile games. Many people who play video games identify as gamers, which can mean anything from someone who enjoys games to someone who is passionate about it. As video games become more social with multiplayer and online capability, gamers find themselves in growing social networks. text: Atomic theory is a scientific concept that describes the composition and structure of matter at its most fundamental level. The number of protons in an atom's nucleus defines its chemical element, with different numbers corresponding to different elements. Atomic theory also explains how atoms interact with each other through chemical bonds, which are formed when electrons are shared or transferred between atoms. This fundamental understanding of atomic structure and behavior has led to significant advancements in various fields, including chemistry, physics, and materials science. It has been estimated that the transition from a mixture of ammonia and dihydrogen molecules to metallic ammonium may occur at pressures just below 25 GPa. It first involved the gradual phasing out, of internal borders under the Schengen agreements, initially in just a handful of Member States. The narrow, central cervical canal runs along its entire length, connecting the uterine cavity and the lumen of the vagina. The lower part of the cervix, known as the vaginal portion of the cervix (or ectocervix), bulges into the top of the vagina. Beaver populations now range across western, central, and eastern Europe, and western Russia and the Scandinavian Peninsula. Small native populations are also present in Mongolia and northwestern China; their numbers were estimated at 150 and 700, respectively, . The numbering is based on the concept of a continuous sequence of years, without regard for the traditional Anno Domini (AD) or Before Christ (BC) notation. This system allows astronomers to easily reference specific dates in their calculations and research, particularly when dealing with ancient astronomical events or observations. They are typically composed of multiple pages, each containing text, images, or other forms of content. The pages are usually arranged in a specific order, with the first page being the beginning of the book and the last page being the end. Books can be categorized into various genres, including fiction, non-fiction, poetry, and reference works. They may also be classified by format, such as hardcover, paperback, or e-book. text: Nakagawa's defenses were centered on Peleliu's highest point, Umurbrogol Mountain, a collection of hills and steep ridges located at the center of Peleliu overlooking a large portion of the island, including the crucial airfield. The Umurbrogol contained some 500 limestone caves, connected by tunnels. Many of these were former mine shafts that were turned into defensive positions. Engineers added sliding armored steel doors with multiple openings to serve both artillery and machine guns. Cave entrances were opened or altered to be slanted as a defense against grenade and flamethrower attacks. The caves and bunkers were connected to a vast tunnel and trench system throughout central Peleliu, which allowed the Japanese to evacuate or reoccupy positions as needed and to take advantage of shrinking interior lines.

text: Advocates of EDP assert that evolutionary psychologists, while acknowledging the role of the environment in shaping behavior and making claims as to its effects, rarely develop explicit models (i.e., predictions of how the environment might shape behavior) to support their claims . EDP seeks to distinguish itself from mainstream evolutionary psychology in this way by embracing a developmental systems approach, and emphasizing that function at one level of organization (e.g., the genetic level) effects organization at adjacent levels of an organization. Developmental systems theorists such as Robert Lickliter point out that the products of development are both genetic and epigenetic, and have questioned the strictly gene-centric view of evolution. However, some authors have rebutted the claim that mainstream evolutionary psychologists do not integrate developmental theory into their theoretical programs, and have further questioned the value of developmental systems theory (see Criticism).

text: Aberration in astronomy refers to the apparent displacement of a star or other celestial object from its true position due to the finite speed of light and the motion of an observer. This phenomenon was first described by James Bradley in 1727, who observed that the position of a star appeared to shift as the Earth moved through its orbit around the Sun. The effect is caused by the difference in time it takes for light to reach an observer from a celestial object when the Earth is moving towards or away from it. As a result, the apparent position of the object appears shifted due to the changing angle of view. Aberration is most pronounced at high declinations and is typically around 20-30 arcseconds in magnitude. It can be corrected for by using trigonometric calculations or by observing the same star at different times of the year when the Earth's motion has changed its position relative to the object.
label: llm

text: Assembler An assembler program creates object code by translating combinations of mnemonics and syntax for operations and addressing modes into their numerical equivalents. This representation typically includes an operation code ("opcode") as well as other control bits and data. The assembler also calculates constant expressions and resolves symbolic names for memory locations and other entities. The use of symbolic references is a key feature of assemblers, saving tedious calculations and manual address updates after program modifications. Most assemblers also include macro facilities for performing textual substitution – e.g., to generate common short sequences of instructions as inline, instead of called subroutines.

text: Instance creation Some algorithms need to create new instances of some ADT (such as new variables, or new stacks). To describe such algorithms, one usually includes in the ADT definition a create() operation that yields an instance of the ADT, usually with axioms equivalent to the result of create() is distinct from any instance already in use by the algorithm. This axiom may be strengthened to exclude also partial aliasing with other instances. For practical use, such as axiom may still allow implementations of create() to yield a previously created instance that has become inaccessible to the program; however, defining that such an instance even is "the same" is difficult, especially in the abstract (though even a re-used block of memory is only "the same object" in certain senses).

text: Much later, during World War I in 1916, a standardized and diluted modification of Labarraque's solution containing hypochlorite (0.5%) and boric acid as an acidic stabilizer was developed by Henry Drysdale Dakin (who gave full credit to Labarraque's prior work in this area). Called Dakin's solution, the method of wound irrigation with chlorinated solutions allowed antiseptic treatment of a wide variety of open wounds, long before the modern antibiotic era. text: Airbus Industrie was initially headquartered in Paris, which is where design, development, flight testing, sales, marketing, and customer support activities were centred; the headquarters was relocated to Toulouse in January 1974. The final assembly line for the A300 was located adjacent to Toulouse Blagnac International Airport. The manufacturing process necessitated transporting each aircraft section being produced by the partner companies scattered across Europe to this one location. The combined use of ferries and roads were used for the assembly of the first A300, however this was time-consuming and not viewed as ideal by Felix Kracht, Airbus Industrie's production director. Kracht's solution was to have the various A300 sections brought to Toulouse by a fleet of Boeing 377-derived Aero Spacelines Super Guppy aircraft, by which means none of the manufacturing sites were more than two hours away. Having the sections airlifted in this manner made the A300 the first airliner to use just-in-time manufacturing techniques, and allowed each company to manufacture its sections as fully equipped, ready-to-fly assemblies.

text: Attila was a king of the Hunnic Empire who ruled from approximately 434 to 453 AD. He is considered one of the most powerful and feared leaders of his time. Attila's early life is not well documented, but it is believed that he rose to power after the death of his brother Bleda in 445 AD. As king, Attila expanded the Hunnic Empire through a series of military campaigns, conquering large areas of Europe and Asia. He was known for his brutal tactics, including massacres and pillaging, which earned him the nickname "the Scourge of God." Attila's empire stretched from modern-day Germany to the Black Sea, and he imposed heavy tributes on the conquered territories. His reign is often associated with a period of significant instability in Europe, as his campaigns disrupted trade and commerce across the continent. Despite his reputation for brutality, some historians have suggested that Attila may have been a more complex figure than previously thought, with some accounts portraying him as a just ruler who protected his people from external threats.

text: Barnabas is a figure in the New Testament of the Bible. He is mentioned as one of the earliest followers of Jesus Christ and is often associated with the apostle Paul. According to the Acts of the Apostles, Barnabas was a Levite from Cyprus who sold his land and brought the proceeds to Jerusalem for the benefit of the early Christian community. The apostle Paul, then known as Saul, was initially met with skepticism by the Jewish Christians in Jerusalem due to his past persecution of them. However, it was Barnabas who intervened on Paul's behalf, vouching for him and introducing him to the other apostles. This led to a partnership between Barnabas and Paul, who together traveled extensively throughout the Mediterranean region, preaching and establishing new Christian communities. The Acts of the Apostles describes their missionary journeys in detail, highlighting the significant role that Barnabas played in supporting and accompanying Paul during this period.

text: Ballads usually are heavily influenced by the regions in which they originate and use the common dialect of the people. Scotland's ballads in particular, both in theme and language, are strongly characterised by their distinctive tradition, even exhibiting some pre-Christian influences in the inclusion of supernatural elements such as travel to the Fairy Kingdom in the Scots ballad "Tam Lin". The ballads do not have any known author or correct version; instead, having been passed down mainly by oral tradition since the Middle Ages, there are many variations of each. The ballads remained an oral tradition until the increased interest in folk songs in the 18th century led collectors such as Bishop Thomas Percy (1729–1811) to publish volumes of popular ballads.

text: Asterix is a French comic book series created by René Goscinny and illustrated by Albert Uderzo. The series follows the adventures of a group of Gauls living in the village of Indomitable Gaul, who are able to resist the Roman occupation due to their access to a magical potion that makes them invulnerable to pain. The main characters include Asterix, Obelix, and Dogmatix, as well as other recurring figures such as Getafix, the druid who brews the potion, and Unhygienix, the village baker. The series is known for its blend of humor, satire, and historical references, often poking fun at French culture and society while also celebrating it. With over 380 million copies sold worldwide, Asterix has become one of the most popular comic book series in history, translated into more than 100 languages and adapted into numerous films, television shows, and stage productions. The series continues to be widely read and enjoyed today, with new albums still being published under the supervision of Albert Uderzo's son, Laurent.

text: The accordion is a portable, hand-held musical instrument that belongs to the aerophone family. It consists of a keyboard or buttonboard on which buttons are pressed by the fingers to produce sound, and a bellows that provides air for the reeds to vibrate. The player's hands operate the keys or buttons while their feet control the bellows, allowing for dynamic expression through varying airflow and pressure. Accordion construction can vary depending on its intended use, with different types including diatonic, chromatic, and bass accordions. Diatonic accordions are typically used in folk music, particularly in European traditions such as polka and waltz styles, while chromatic accordions offer a wider range of tonal possibilities and are often employed in classical and popular music settings. The instrument's versatility has led to its adoption in various musical genres worldwide, including traditional, folk, and contemporary styles.

text: Often, if the skill is being used in a common or practical way, people will consider it a craft instead of art. Likewise, if the skill is being used in a commercial or industrial way, it may be considered commercial art instead of fine art. On the other hand, crafts and design are sometimes considered applied art. Some art followers have argued that the difference between fine art and applied art has more to do with value judgments made about the art than any clear definitional difference. However, even fine art often has goals beyond pure creativity and self-expression. The purpose of works of art may be to communicate ideas, such as in politically, spiritually, or philosophically motivated art; to create a sense of beauty (see aesthetics); to explore the nature of perception; for pleasure; or to generate strong emotions. The purpose may also be seemingly nonexistent.

text: The bokken is a traditional Japanese wooden sword used for martial arts training and practice. It is typically made from a single piece of wood, often Paulownia or White Oak, which is prized for its strength and durability. The bokken's design and construction are intended to mimic the appearance and weight of a katana, Japan's curved sword, allowing practitioners to develop their technique and skills in a safe and controlled environment. In contrast to a real sword, the bokken is blunt and lacks an edge, making it suitable for training without the risk of injury or damage. Bokkens are often used by practitioners of kendo, kenjutsu, and other Japanese martial arts disciplines as a tool for honing their technique, building strength and endurance, and developing their mental focus and discipline. The use of bokken has been an integral part of Japanese martial arts training for centuries, providing a safe and effective means to develop the skills necessary for proficiency with a real sword.

text: An autobiography is a written account of an individual's life, typically composed by the person themselves. The genre has been popularized by notable figures such as politicians, artists, and historical leaders, who have used autobiographies to share their perspectives on major events and issues. While some autobiographies are written in a straightforward and factual style, others may employ literary techniques, such as narrative devices and rhetorical flourishes, to convey the author's personality and emotions. text: Vulnerabilities AIM had security weaknesses that have enabled exploits to be created that used third-party software to perform malicious acts on users' computers. These messages could easily be mistaken as coming from a friend and contain a link to a Web address that installed software on the user's computer to restart the cycle.

text: The Apache Software Foundation is a non-profit organization that oversees and maintains a wide range of open-source software projects. The foundation's mission is to provide a neutral environment for the development and distribution of free and open-source software, with a focus on community-driven projects that are governed by collaborative decision-making processes. The Apache Software Foundation is responsible for managing over 350 top-level projects, including popular applications such as Apache Kafka, Apache Cassandra, and Apache Hadoop, which are used in various industries and domains. The foundation's governance model emphasizes transparency, accountability, and meritocracy, with decisions made through a consensus-driven process among its members. text: The history of Cambodia spans over two millennia, with the earliest known civilization emerging in the 1st century AD. The Funan Kingdom, a major trading hub, flourished from the 1st to the 6th century before being succeeded by the Chenla Empire. In the 9th century, the Khmer Empire rose to prominence under King Jayavarman II, who declared independence from Java and established Angkor as the capital. The Khmer Empire reached its peak during the reign of King Suryavarman II in the 12th century, with the construction of the magnificent temple complex of Angkor Wat. However, the empire declined in the 15th century due to internal conflicts and external invasions. In the 19th century, Cambodia became a French protectorate, which lasted until its independence was declared in 1953. The country then experienced a tumultuous period under the rule of Prince Norodom Sihanouk, followed by the Khmer Rouge regime led by Pol Pot, which resulted in widespread human rights abuses and the deaths of an estimated 1.7 to 2.2 million people between 1975 and 1979.

text: Burkina Faso is a landlocked country located in West Africa. It borders several countries including Mali to the north, Niger to the northeast, Benin and Togo to the southeast, Ghana and Côte d'Ivoire to the south, and Mauritania to the northwest. The capital city of Burkina Faso is Ouagadougou, which is also the country's largest city. The country has a diverse geography, with savannas in the north and south, and a plateau region in the center. The climate varies from tropical in the south to semi-arid in the north. The population of Burkina Faso is approximately 23 million people, with over 60 ethnic groups represented. The official languages are French and Mooré, but many other languages are also spoken. The country has a mixed economy, with agriculture being the mainstay of the economy, followed by mining and manufacturing. Burkina Faso gained independence from France in 1960 and has since been a republic, with a president serving as head of state and government.

The South Africans aimed to prevent a MPLA victory, which would have led to a communist government in Angola. The operation involved air and ground attacks on MPLA forces, as well as support for FNLA rebels. text: Local and tribal government Botswana's government is centralised, and the powers of local government are determined by national law. Smaller towns also have town councils, while rural areas are collected under district councils. These councils are primarily responsible for education, health, roads, water, local development, and general welfare. Local governmental bodies do not collect significant funds, and nearly all local spending is funded by the national government.

Corticosteroid treatment may be required for life. Antibiotics can be used to treat bacterial overgrowth. With a very low serum albumin, transfusion with blood plasma or an infusion of hetastarch may be necessary to treat the signs until the diet can take effect. Lymphangiectasia is rarely cured but can remain in remission for a long time. It can be fatal when unresponsive to treatment.

text: The American Revolution was a pivotal event in modern history that took place from 1765 to 1783. The revolution was sparked by growing tensions between the Thirteen Colonies and Great Britain, fueled by issues such as taxation without representation, territorial expansion, and economic restrictions. Key events included the Boston Massacre, the Boston Tea Party, and the signing of the Declaration of Independence in 1776. The Continental Army, led by George Washington, fought against the British Army and its allies, with significant battles taking place at Lexington and Concord, Bunker Hill, Saratoga, and Yorktown. After a long and bloody conflict, the Treaty of Paris was signed in 1783, formally ending the war and recognizing American independence from Great Britain. The revolution had far-reaching consequences, shaping the course of modern democracy and influencing subsequent movements for self-governance around the world.

text: Birds live and breed in most terrestrial habitats and on all seven continents, reaching their southern extreme in the snow petrel's breeding colonies up to inland in Antarctica. The highest bird diversity occurs in tropical regions. It was earlier thought that this high diversity was the result of higher speciation rates in the tropics; however studies from the 2000s found higher speciation rates in the high latitudes that were offset by greater extinction rates than in the tropics. Many species migrate annually over great distances and across oceans; several families of birds have adapted to life both on the world's oceans and in them, and some seabird species come ashore only to breed, while some penguins have been recorded diving up to deep.

text: The nitrogen atom features a lone electron pair that can bind H+ to form an ammonium ion R3NH+. The lone electron pair is represented in this article by a two dots above or next to the N. The water solubility of simple amines is enhanced by hydrogen bonding involving these lone electron pairs. Typically salts of ammonium compounds exhibit the following order of solubility in water: primary ammonium () > secondary ammonium () > tertiary ammonium (R3NH+). Small aliphatic amines display significant solubility in many solvents, whereas those with large substituents are lipophilic. Aromatic amines, such as aniline, have their lone pair electrons conjugated into the benzene ring, thus their tendency to engage in hydrogen bonding is diminished. Their boiling points are high and their solubility in water is low.

text: Alexios V Doukas was a Byzantine emperor who ruled from 1341 to 1347. He was the son of Andronikos III Palaiologos and Anna of Savoy, and his reign marked the beginning of the end of the Byzantine Empire's stability. Alexios V initially supported the regency of his mother, but he soon turned against her and seized power for himself. His early years as emperor were marked by a series of military campaigns against the Ottoman Turks, who had been making significant gains in the region. Despite some initial successes, Alexios V was ultimately unable to stem the tide of Ottoman expansion, and his empire began to fragment under the pressure of external threats. The Black Death also ravaged the Byzantine Empire during his reign, further weakening its already fragile economy and social structures. As a result, Alexios V's rule was marked by instability and decline, setting the stage for the eventual fall of Constantinople in 1453.

Capp continued to write and draw Li'l Abner until 1977, when he retired the character due to declining circulation and his own health issues. Throughout his career, Al Capp was known for his unique blend of humor, social commentary, and caricature, which often tackled topics such as poverty, racism, and government corruption. He also created other notable characters, including Sadie Hawkins and Shmoo, and wrote several novels and essays. text: Date The prophet Jonah is mentioned in 2 Kings 14:25, which places Jonah's life during the reign of Jeroboam II (786–746 BC), but the book of Jonah itself does not name a king or give any other details that would give the story a firm date. The majority of scholars date the book much later, to the post-exilic period sometime between the late 5th to early 4th century BC; perhaps (along with Book of Ruth) as a counter to the emphasis on racial purity in the time of Ezra. An even later date is sometimes proposed, with Katherine Dell arguing for the Hellenistic period (332–167 BC).

He won reelection in 1928, and again in 1932, making him the only governor elected to three terms. He served from January 4, 1925 to March 13, 1933. text: Apoptosis is a naturally occurring process of programmed cell death that plays a crucial role in the development and maintenance of multicellular organisms. It is a highly regulated mechanism that allows for the elimination of damaged or unwanted cells, thereby preventing tissue damage and promoting overall health. During apoptosis, a series of cellular changes occur, including chromatin condensation, DNA fragmentation, and membrane blebbing, ultimately leading to cell shrinkage and fragmentation. This process can be triggered by various internal and external signals, such as DNA damage, oxidative stress, or hormonal changes, and is mediated by a complex interplay of molecular pathways involving proteins, lipids, and other cellular components. Apoptosis is essential for the normal functioning of tissues and organs, including the elimination of excess cells during embryonic development, the maintenance of tissue homeostasis, and the prevention of cancer. Dysregulation of apoptosis has been implicated in various diseases, including cancer, neurodegenerative disorders, and autoimmune diseases.

text: An antidepressant is a type of medication designed to treat depression and related mood disorders. These medications work by altering the levels and activity of certain neurotransmitters in the brain, such as serotonin and norepinephrine, which are involved in regulating mood, appetite, and sleep. Antidepressants can be classified into several categories based on their mechanism of action, including selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs), serotonin-norepinephrine reuptake inhibitors (SNRIs), tricyclic antidepressants (TCAs), and monoamine oxidase inhibitors (MAOIs). The exact mechanisms by which antidepressants exert their effects are not fully understood, but research suggests that they may involve changes in gene expression, neuroplasticity, and the regulation of stress response systems. Antidepressants can be effective in reducing symptoms of depression, such as low mood, loss of interest in activities, and changes in appetite or sleep patterns, allowing individuals to function more normally and improve their overall quality of life.

text: In 1948, the ACLU prevailed in the McCollum v. Board of Education case, which challenged public school religious classes taught by clergy paid for by private funds. In the 1960s, the ACLU, in response to member insistence, turned its attention to the in-class promotion of religion. In 1960, 42 percent of American schools included Bible reading. In 1962, the ACLU published a policy statement condemning in-school prayers, observation of religious holidays, and Bible reading. The Supreme Court concurred with the ACLU's position when it prohibited New York's in-school prayers in the 1962 Engel v. Vitale decision. Religious factions across the country rebelled against the anti-prayer decisions, leading them to propose the School Prayer Constitutional Amendment, which declared in-school prayer legal. text: The Amazon River is a major watercourse in South America that flows through seven countries, including Brazil, Peru, Colombia, Venezuela, Ecuador, Bolivia, and Guyana. The Amazon River originates from the Andes Mountains in Peru and flows approximately 6,400 kilometers to its mouth at the Atlantic Ocean in Brazil. Along its course, it passes through a vast network of tributaries, including the Ucayali, Marañón, and Madeira rivers, which contribute significantly to its volume. The Amazon River basin is home to an estimated 40,000 plant species, 1,500 bird species, and over 3,000 fish species, making it one of the most biodiverse regions on Earth. text: The Mackerras pendulum takes the TPP majorities of all electorates and arranges them in order, from the seat with the highest government majority to the seat with the highest opposition majority. For example, ahead of the 2007 election, Labor needed to win a minimum of 16 additional seats to form a government, and the 16th-weakest government seat (McMillan) had a TPP majority of 4.9 points. Thus, the pendulum predicted that Labor would need a uniform TPP swing of 4.9 points to win the 2007 election. Labor in fact gained a swing of 5.6 points, which the pendulum had predicted would result in 21 additional Labor seats under a uniform swing. In fact, Labor gained 23 seats, and not all seats that changed hands were those with the slimmest Coalition majorities, because swings in each district are unique and not uniform.

Some sources suggest that he may have been involved in a plot to overthrow Emperor Gratian (367-383 AD) or that he was forced into retirement due to his advanced age. The Constitutional Court ensures that laws comply with the Constitution, while the Supreme Court handles appeals and disputes. text: Whereas a biotic stress would include living disturbances such as fungi or harmful insects, abiotic stress factors, or stressors, are naturally occurring, often intangible and inanimate factors such as intense sunlight, temperature or wind that may cause harm to the plants and animals in the area affected. Abiotic stress is essentially unavoidable. Abiotic stress affects animals, but plants are especially dependent, if not solely dependent, on environmental factors, so it is particularly constraining. Abiotic stress is the most harmful factor concerning the growth and productivity of crops worldwide. Research has also shown that abiotic stressors are at their most harmful when they occur together, in combinations of abiotic stress factors.

text: Psychodynamic theory posits that anxiety is often the result of opposing unconscious wishes or fears that manifest via maladaptive defense mechanisms (such as suppression, repression, anticipation, regression, somatization, passive aggression, dissociation) that develop to adapt to problems with early objects (e.g., caregivers) and empathic failures in childhood. For example, persistent parental discouragement of anger may result in repression/suppression of angry feelings which manifests as gastrointestinal distress (somatization) when provoked by another while the anger remains unconscious and outside the individual's awareness. Such conflicts can be targets for successful treatment with psychodynamic therapy. While psychodynamic therapy tends to explore the underlying roots of anxiety, cognitive behavioral therapy has also been shown to be a successful treatment for anxiety by altering irrational thoughts and unwanted behaviors.

However, while Lamentations is generically similar to the Sumerian laments of the early 2nd millennium BCE (e.g., "Lamentation over the Destruction of Ur," "Lament for Sumer and Ur," "Nippur Lament"), the Sumerian laments (that we have) were recited on the occasion of the rebuilding of a temple, so their story has a happy ending, whereas the book of Lamentations was written before the return/rebuilding, and thus contains only lamentations and pleas to God with no response or resolution.

text: NTLM is a suite of security protocols developed by Microsoft for authenticating users and computers on a network. The acronym NTLM stands for New Technology LAN Manager. It was introduced in the 1990s as an improvement over earlier authentication methods, such as WDigest and SPNEGO. NTLM uses a challenge-response mechanism to verify user credentials, where the server sends a random number to the client, which then responds with a hashed version of the username and password. The server verifies this response against its own stored hash values. NTLM supports multiple authentication protocols, including NTLMv1, NTLMv2, and NTLMSSP. It is widely used in Windows-based networks for authenticating users to access shared resources, such as files and printers. However, NTLM has been criticized for its vulnerability to password cracking and other security threats, leading some organizations to adopt more secure authentication methods, such as Kerberos or Active Directory Federation Services.

text: Theoretical understanding of condensed matter physics is closely related to the notion of emergence, wherein complex assemblies of particles behave in ways dramatically different from their individual constituents. For example, a range of phenomena related to high temperature superconductivity are understood poorly, although the microscopic physics of individual electrons and lattices is well known. Similarly, models of condensed matter systems have been studied where collective excitations behave like photons and electrons, thereby describing electromagnetism as an emergent phenomenon. Emergent properties can also occur at the interface between materials: one example is the lanthanum aluminate-strontium titanate interface, where two band-insulators are joined to create conductivity and superconductivity.

text: APL is a programming language developed in the 1960s by Kenneth E. Iverson at IBM. It was designed to be a concise and expressive language for mathematical and scientific computing. The language also employs a unique concept called "array processing," where data is represented as multidimensional arrays that can be manipulated using a variety of built-in functions. APL's syntax is often described as "read-only" because it requires the programmer to understand the underlying mathematical concepts, rather than simply memorizing keywords and syntax rules. Despite its unusual notation, APL has been widely used in various fields, including finance, engineering, and scientific research, due to its ability to express complex mathematical operations in a concise and efficient manner. text: Antisemitism is a form of prejudice and hostility towards Jewish people, often rooted in stereotypes and misconceptions about their culture, religion, or identity. In the Middle Ages, Jews were subjected to persecution and violence, including forced conversions and expulsions from various countries. During the 20th century, antisemitism contributed to the Holocaust, in which six million Jews were systematically murdered by the Nazi regime. Today, antisemitism persists in many parts of the world, manifesting in hate speech, vandalism, and violent attacks against Jewish individuals or communities. Antisemitic ideologies often overlap with other forms of prejudice, such as racism and xenophobia, and can be fueled by conspiracy theories and misinformation about Jews and their role in society. text: In counterpoint, the functional independence of voices is the prime concern. The violation of this principle leads to special effects, which are avoided in counterpoint. In organ registers, certain interval combinations and chords are activated by a single key so that playing a melody results in parallel voice leading. These voices, losing independence, are fused into one and the parallel chords are perceived as single tones with a new timbre. This effect is also used in orchestral arrangements; for instance, in Ravel’s Bolero #5 the parallel parts of flutes, horn and celesta resemble the sound of an electric organ. In counterpoint, parallel voices are prohibited because they violate the homogeneity of musical texture when independent voices occasionally disappear turning into a new timbre quality and vice versa.

text: After Israeli independence For most of its history in the modern era, the Israeli wine industry was based predominantly on the production of Kosher wines which were exported worldwide to Jewish communities. It was not until the 1980s that the industry at large saw a revival in quality winemaking, when an influx of winemaking talent from Australia, California and France brought modern technology and technical know-how to the growing Israeli wine industry. In 1989, the first boutique winery in Israel, Margalit Winery, was founded. By the 1990s, Israeli estates such as Golan Heights Winery and Domaine du Castel were winning awards at international wine competitions. In the United States, the selective initiation of low-dose aspirin, based on an individualised assessment, has been deemed reasonable for the primary prevention of cardiovascular disease in people aged between 40 and 59 who have a 10% or greater risk of developing cardiovascular disease over the next 10 years and are not at an increased risk of bleeding.
